headline: E-Invoicing Under GST in India: Practical Compliance Guide
h1: E-Invoicing Under GST in India: Practical Compliance Guide
title: E-Invoicing Under GST in India: Rules and Compliance
desc: Know who must issue GST e-invoices in India, what documents are covered, how IRN and QR code work, and key compliance points.
lede: E-invoicing under GST is mandatory for notified registered persons, currently most taxpayers with aggregate turnover above Rs 5 crore, subject to CBIC notifications and exemptions. The supplier must upload invoice data to an Invoice Registration Portal, obtain an IRN and QR code, and issue the authenticated invoice as per CGST Rule 48.
body:
Who must issue e-invoices under GST?

E-invoicing is mandatory for registered persons covered by CBIC notifications, currently most taxpayers with aggregate turnover above Rs 5 crore in any financial year from 2017-18 onwards, as per CGST Rule 48(4) read with CBIC Notification No. 13/2020-Central Tax, as amended.

Aggregate turnover is computed on an all-India basis under Section 2(6) of the CGST Act and includes taxable, exempt and export supplies, while excluding GST and certain inward reverse charge supplies. Certain persons and supplies are excluded by CBIC notifications and CGST Rules, such as SEZ units, banks, NBFCs, financial institutions, insurance companies, passenger transport services, GTA services to unregistered recipients, and other notified categories.

What documents are covered?

For notified taxpayers, e-invoicing covers tax invoices for B2B taxable supplies, exports, SEZ supplies, and debit notes and credit notes issued for such supplies, as per CGST Rule 48(4) read with Rule 53A. B2C retail invoices are generally not required to be reported as e-invoices unless the supply falls within the notified class.

How is an e-invoice generated?

An e-invoice is not created by the GST portal; the supplier prepares invoice data in the prescribed e-invoice schema and uploads it to an Invoice Registration Portal, as per CGST Rule 48(4) and FORM GST INV-01. The IRP validates the details, checks for duplicates, and issues an Invoice Reference Number with a QR code, as per CGST Rule 48(5).

The IRN and QR code must be included on the invoice for it to be treated as a valid e-invoice, as per CGST Rule 48(4). For movement of goods, e-invoice details can be used for e-way bill generation where an e-way bill is otherwise required under CGST Rule 138.

What are the timing and correction rules?

Generate the IRN before reporting the invoice in GSTR-1 and, as a practical control, before goods are moved or services are billed; IRN cannot be generated after 30 days from the invoice date, as per CGST Rule 48(10). If an IRN needs cancellation, it can generally be cancelled on the IRP within 24 hours, subject to conditions in CGST Rule 48(11), including whether the details have already been used for an e-way bill or passed to the GST system.

Corrected invoices, credit notes and debit notes should be reflected in the applicable GST returns under the CGST Act and CGST Rules, because mismatches can affect the recipient's ITC position under Section 16(2)(a) of the CGST Act.

What should businesses check before relying on e-invoicing?

Businesses should maintain a GSTIN-wise applicability matrix, turnover computation, notified exemption status, software mapping, and document numbering discipline. The e-invoice requirements are issued through CBIC notifications and CGST Rules, and thresholds or exemptions may change, so confirm the current position before each financial year and before adding new GSTINs.
faqs: Is e-invoicing mandatory for all GST registered persons?
No. It applies to notified registered persons, currently most taxpayers with aggregate turnover above Rs 5 crore, subject to CBIC notifications and exemptions under CGST Rule 48.
Does GST e-invoicing apply to B2C invoices?
Generally no. E-invoicing covers notified B2B taxable supplies, exports, SEZ supplies, and related debit notes and credit notes as per CGST Rule 48 and Rule 53A.
What happens if IRN is not generated when required?
The invoice may not be treated as a valid e-invoice under CGST Rule 48(4), and it may affect the recipient's ITC under Section 16(2)(a) of the CGST Act and attract consequences under the CGST Act.
source: This article is general information, not advice. Tax rules and figures change — confirm current provisions with the official portals (incometax.gov.in / gst.gov.in) or contact us before acting.
